locked_facts: Horizontal velocity is constant throughout (zero horizontal acceleration)
Vertical acceleration = g = 9.8 m/s² downward always
Trajectory is a parabola: y = x·tan(θ) - gx²/(2u²cos²θ)
Range R = u²sin(2θ)/g, maximum at θ = 45°
vx = u·cos(θ) at all times, vy = u·sin(θ) - gt
truth_statements: The horizontal velocity of a projectile remains constant as there is no horizontal acceleration
The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated with acceleration g directed downward
The trajectory of a projectile is a parabola
Maximum range is achieved at 45° angle of projection
Two angles of projection θ and (90°-θ) give the same range

aha_moment.what_to_show: Projectile at several points along the trajectory with velocity components resolved — horizontal arrow stays the same length at every point while vertical arrow shrinks to zero at the peak and reverses direction on the way down
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Constant-length horizontal velocity arrows versus changing vertical velocity arrows at multiple trajectory points
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see with their own eyes that the horizontal component never changes — gravity only acts vertically

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Projectile on the ground with initial velocity vector shown at the launch angle. The vector is decomposed into horizontal (vx) and vertical (vy) components with labels.
simulation_states.emphasis: Show the full velocity vector and its two components clearly — horizontal and vertical

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Projectile launches and traces a parabolic path. At 3-4 points along the trajectory, velocity component arrows are drawn. Horizontal arrow remains constant, vertical arrow shrinks as it rises.
simulation_states.emphasis: The horizontal arrow length does not change even as the projectile rises — only the vertical component changes

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: At the peak, vy = 0 but vx is still full strength. Descending side shows vy growing downward while vx remains unchanged. All arrows displayed simultaneously for comparison.
simulation_states.emphasis: Horizontal velocity arrow constant length at every single point — this is the core insight

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive mode: student adjusts launch angle and speed. Range, height, and time of flight update in real time. Complementary angles (e.g. 30° and 60°) produce the same range.
simulation_states.emphasis: Try θ = 45° for maximum range. Try complementary angles to see equal range.

common_misconceptions.belief: Horizontal velocity decreases like vertical velocity — the ball slows down in both directions
trigger_phrases: horizontal mein bhi gravity lagti hai
dono direction mein slow hota hai
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says horizontal velocity remains constant because there is no horizontal acceleration — gravity only acts vertically downward
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show constant-length horizontal velocity arrows at every point of the trajectory while vertical arrows change

common_traps: forgetting to resolve initial velocity into components
sign errors in vy when projectile descends
